content.problemStatement:
Chef has an array AAA containing NNN non-negative integers.

He will perform a sequence of operations on this array.

The XXX-th operation is as follows:


For each index iii (1‚Č§i‚Č§N1 \leq i \leq N1‚Č§i‚Č§N), either leave AiA_iAi‚Äč unchanged, or replace AiA_iAi‚Äč with Ai‚ą£XA_i \mid XAi‚Äč‚ą£X.

The choice is made independently for each index.

Here, ‚ą£\mid‚ą£ denotes the bitwise OR operation.
Find the minimum number of operations needed for Chef to be able to obtain an array that's sorted in non-decreasing order, i.e. for

A1‚Č§A2‚Č§‚Ä¶‚Č§ANA_1 \leq A_2 \leq\ldots\leq A_NA1‚Äč‚Č§A2‚Äč‚Č§‚Ä¶‚Č§AN‚Äč to hold.

It can be proved that the answer is finite.

If the array is already sorted, output 000.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN ‚ÄĒ the length of the array.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,‚Ä¶,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1‚Äč,A2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äč.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the minimum number of operations needed to obtain a sorted array.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2‚čÖ1052\cdot 10^52‚čÖ105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: One optimal sequence of operations is as follows.

Initially, A=[2,1,3]A = [2, 1, 3]A=[2,1,3].
First, we have X=1X = 1X=1.
Leave all elements unchanged, so that A=[2,1,3]A = [2, 1, 3]A=[2,1,3] still.
Next, we have X=2X = 2X=2.
Leave A1A_1A1‚Äč and A3A_3A3‚Äč unchanged, and replace A2A_2A2‚Äč by A2‚ą£XA_2 \mid XA2‚Äč‚ą£X.
This will make the array [2,3,3][2, 3, 3][2,3,3], which is sorted.

Test case 222: The array is already sorted.
Test case 333: A=[9,8,9,15,14]A = [9, 8, 9, 15, 14]A=[9,8,9,15,14].
For X=1X = 1X=1, replace A2A_2A2‚Äč and A5A_5A5‚Äč by their bitwise ORs with XXX, to obtain [9,9,9,15,15][9, 9, 9, 15, 15][9,9,9,15,15] which is sorted.
